items.q: Which Spanish 'you' do you use with a stranger or elderly person?
items.why: Students use tú with everyone like English 'you'. Spanish distinguishes FORMAL (usted) for politeness/distance and INFORMAL (tú) for friends/family. Usted takes third-person verb forms, which trips students up.

items.q: Why does 'qué' have an accent in '¿Qué quieres?' but not always elsewhere?
items.correct: The accent distinguishes interrogative/exclamative 'qué' from relative 'que'.
items.why: Students see two spellings of the 'same word'. Spanish uses written accents to distinguish homophones with different functions: qué (question/exclamation) vs que (that/which - relative).

items.q: What goes between 'veo' and 'mi amigo' in 'I see my friend'?
items.why: Students omit the personal a. When the direct object is a SPECIFIC PERSON (or pet), Spanish inserts 'a' before it. Omitting it sounds odd. 'Veo a Juan' (not 'Veo Juan').

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my sister'?
items.correct: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.misconception: Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.
items.random_wrong: Yo llevo bien con mi hermana.
items.why: Correct answer: Me llevo bien con mi hermana.. “Me llevo bueno con mi hermana.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is the correct use of ser/estar: 'I am tired'?
items.why: Correct answer: Estoy cansado.. “Soy cansado.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many forms does the Spanish adjective 'simpático' have (masc sing, masc plur, fem sing, fem plur)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 forms. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
